artifacts.content:
Hi Sam,

Kids are finally down and James is marking labs, so I’m grabbing ten quiet minutes to actually write instead of just sending you random Reels.

Work has felt like walking a tightrope the last couple of years. I keep describing Maple Crest as a “plain old civil/structural consultancy,” but you and I both know how much that really changed after Dad’s tax audit back in March 2022. Sitting in that Canada Revenue Agency Kitchener office while they went through those three design–build plant retrofits from 2019–2021 line by line was enough to cure me of any appetite for fancy industrial work. Since then I’ve been steering us hard toward low‑risk municipal stuff in Kitchener and Waterloo – roads, intersections, storm ponds – anything where we’re not carrying design–build risk or weird revenue‑recognition questions.

It’s not just the CRA thing, either. Those three slow quarters in 2020–21 when we maxed the line of credit just to make payroll are still in my bones. I can feel the $14,000/month debt‑servicing payment every time someone suggests we “stretch” for a big industrial retrofit. Steady cash flow from municipal work is literally what’s keeping the lights on.

Layer on top of that you being on bed rest since January and Carlos still struggling with the back injury, and I’ve basically become the bottleneck for every structural review. I’ve pulled back on higher‑risk field inspections because I just don’t trust our bench strength enough to be everywhere at once.

Anyway, this is a long way of saying: I miss when you were across the glass wall rolling your eyes with me about drainage reports instead of me overthinking CRA memos alone at 10 p.m.

Text me when you’re awake enough for gossip that isn’t about blood pressure.

Laura

metadata.subject: Catching you up on the circus

artifacts.content:
Hi Erin,

I’ve been staring at the renewal spreadsheets and thinking about how much of our “strategy” is just the fallout from people getting sick or hurt.

Carlos is the obvious one. Ever since his non‑work‑related L4–L5 disc herniation in November 2024 – the one from lifting that stupid furniture at home – we’ve been dancing around his limitations. His physiatrist at Grand River Sports & Spine and the folks at KW Rehabilitation keep saying “partial to full recovery in 6–9 months” if he sticks with twice‑weekly physio, core work, no lifting over 10 kg, etc., but every report also comes with another line about permanent sit‑stand accommodations and no more full‑day field work in Cambridge or Guelph.

On our side, we’ve cobbled together a plan: adjustable sit‑stand workstation, 2–3 hours/day of CAD to start, micro‑breaks every 30–45 minutes. Sun Life, meanwhile, is dragging their heels on the life waiver. They acknowledged the application back on January 17, 2025 and then immediately asked Dr. Patel at Grand River for more documentation about the post‑surgery complications. So we’re still sending out that $186.40/month life premium from payroll while we wait.

Samantha’s file is its own saga – high‑risk pregnancy, bed rest since January 19, 2025, OB at Grand River saying prognosis is good but no billable work until at least mid‑September. Manulife won’t finalize her life waiver either; they want the full obstetrics report from the St. Mary’s high‑risk clinic before they’ll even look at waiving premiums.

None of this fits neatly into a benefits brochure blurb about “supportive culture,” but it’s the reality we’re managing. Let’s block an hour next week to map out how these restrictions actually play against project staffing for summer.

Laura

metadata.subject: Carlos, Sam, and the benefits dominoes

artifacts.content:
OneNote – Private journal

Title: Why I keep pushing municipal work
Date: 2025-01-05

I keep coming back to the same three anchors whenever I question whether I’m being too conservative with Maple Crest’s project mix:

1) CRA – March 2022. Sitting in that bland CRA Kitchener office while they dissected the revenue recognition on those three 2019–2021 design–build retrofits. Watching Dad’s hands actually shake when they started talking about “aggressive assumptions.” I promised myself in that room we’d stop flirting with quasi‑contractor risk and stick to clean consulting on municipal work in Kitchener and Waterloo.

2) LOC – 2020–21. Three brutal quarters where we maxed the line of credit just to cover payroll. $14,000/month in debt‑servicing ever since. Municipal roads, culverts, and stormwater ponds may be boring, but they pay on time and keep that payment from swallowing us.

3) Safety + capacity – 2024–25. Samantha on doctor‑ordered bed rest after the January 19, 2025 high‑risk pregnancy diagnosis. Carlos off since November 3, 2024 with the herniated lumbar disc he got moving furniture, bouncing between Grand River Sports & Spine and KW Rehab. I’ve already pulled back on higher‑risk field inspections because I don’t want a near miss on a site we’re barely staffed to cover.

None of this shows up on a glossy “nature of business” line, but it’s why I keep saying no to sexy industrial retrofits and cannabis facilities and yes to yet another intersection in south Kitchener.

metadata.title: Why I keep pushing municipal work

artifacts.content:
Hi Mom,

Quick update before I run Ethan to soccer.

Dad’s doing a bit better this week, but I can still see how much the November 2024 heart attack and all those trips to St. Mary’s General have taken out of him. When his cardiologist told us in that November appointment that his prognosis was “favourable if he keeps up rehab three times a week and avoids stressful site visits,” it was like a line got drawn for both of us. I’ve pulled him off all client‑facing work and he’s just doing the odd design review from the house in Waterloo now.

At the same time, I’m still trying to dig us out from the pandemic years. Those three slow quarters in 2020–21 when we maxed the line of credit to cover payroll are still hanging over my head; the $14,000/month debt‑servicing doesn’t care that Dad’s heart is fragile or that municipal clients want everything faster and cheaper.

Layer on top of that my own back nonsense from the March 18, 2023 armoire incident and the stress leave in October 2023 when Dr. Nguyen at the Kitchener Downtown Community Health Centre basically said, “45–50 hours a week or you’re going to collapse again,” and it feels like the whole family’s medical chart is stapled to Maple Crest’s balance sheet.

I know you keep offering to take more of the after‑school load with Ethan and Chloe so James can stay late at Grand River Collegiate for tutoring and Michael can work late in Cambridge. Please don’t burn yourself out too.

We’re still aiming for Sunday dinner in Waterloo. I’ll bring dessert if you handle the roast.

Love,
Laura

metadata.subject: Dad, work, and everything in between

artifacts.content:
Event: Staffing & accommodations planning – Sam + Carlos
Date: 2025-02-11
Time: 9:00–10:30 a.m.
Location: Maple Crest Engineering – Boardroom (3rd floor)
Attendees: Laura, Erin, Michael (first 30 min)

Description/Notes:

Agenda
1. Samantha Lee – high‑risk pregnancy leave
   • Off work since 2025-01-19 per OB at Grand River Hospital; on modified bed rest at home.
   • Prognosis from March 4, 2025 appointment: very good; expected to carry to term and make full recovery postpartum.
   • Tentative RTW: mid‑September 2025 on gradual schedule (approx. 60% hours for first month; no field inspections or overnight travel for ~3 months postpartum per St. Mary’s high‑risk note).
   • No life waiver of premium approved to date; Manulife letter Feb 3, 2025 says they’re waiting on full obstetrics report from St. Mary’s high‑risk clinic; Maple Crest continues remitting her life premiums in full.

2. Carlos Mendoza – lumbar disc herniation
   • Off since 2024-11-03 after non‑work‑related lower back injury (herniated L4–L5 disc lifting furniture at home).
   • Prognosis (Waterloo Sports & Spine + Grand River physiatrist): partial to full recovery within 6–9 months with twice‑weekly physio, strict lifting limits (no >10 kg), likely permanent sit‑stand accommodation and no prolonged field work; may never tolerate >3–4 hours of static sitting.
   • Life waiver application submitted Jan 2025; Sun Life acknowledged Jan 17 and requested additional medical from Dr. Patel at Grand River. Premiums still being paid as usual until decision.

3. Project resourcing – spring/summer construction season
   • Reassign Sam’s subdivision files; limit Carlos to office‑based CAD when he returns (2–3 hrs/day initially).

Hi James,

I know we mostly talk about this stuff in the kitchen, but I wanted to put some of it in writing so I don’t lose the thread between lunches and site visits.

I’ve been thinking a lot about how many different directions I’m being pulled in: running a firm that’s still paying $14k/month on the line of credit we maxed in 2020–21, trying to keep Dad away from stressful site work after St. Mary’s told us his prognosis is “favourable if he sticks to rehab and avoids field visits,” worrying about Samantha on bed rest since January 19, 2025 with the high‑risk pregnancy, and Carlos bouncing between Grand River Sports & Spine and KW Rehab since his November 3, 2024 back injury.

On top of that, my own back hasn’t been the same since I herniated the L4–L5 disc moving that armoire in the living room on March 18, 2023. Dr. Chow keeps warning me not to go back to 60–70 hour weeks, and Dr. Nguyen at the downtown clinic basically said my prognosis is “good as long as you keep it to 45–50 hours and keep doing monthly check‑ins through December 2025.”

I’m proud of the way we’ve looked after people – ordering Carlos the sit‑stand station, making sure Samantha’s life coverage stays active while Manulife dithers about the waiver, even covering Carlos’s share of his life premium out of my own pocket since his STD ended in November – but some nights it feels like I’m holding everything together with duct tape and caffeine.

Thank you for quietly taking on the morning drives to Grand River Collegiate and the late‑day soccer practices so I can leave when my back starts screaming. I know it means more prep and less downtime for you.

Pizza and movie night still on for Friday. Let’s not talk about benefits for 24 hours.

L

OneNote – Strategy / Benefits scratchpad
Date: 2025-02-05

Things I don’t say out loud in broker meetings:

• Nature of business on paper is “civil/structural consulting,” but in practice it’s “whatever keeps CRA off our backs and services the $14,000/month LOC we took on to cover payroll in those three awful 2020–21 quarters.” Since the March 2022 audit at the CRA Kitchener office over the three 2019–2021 design–build plant retrofits, I’ve been deliberately overweighting Kitchener/Waterloo municipal work.

• People behind the claim numbers:
   – Samantha Lee: Senior Civil Engineer, off since 2025‑01‑19 with severe gestational hypertension/high‑risk pregnancy. OB at Grand River + St. Mary’s high‑risk clinic both say prognosis is good, full recovery postpartum, tentative RTW mid‑Sept 2025 at ~60% hours. No life waiver approved; coverage continues as active, we keep remitting premiums.
   – Carlos Mendoza: CAD Tech, off since 2024‑11‑03 with non‑work‑related L4–L5 disc herniation from lifting furniture at home. Physio/physiatry consensus: 6–9 months to partial/full recovery, permanent lifting restriction, sit‑stand desk, max 3–4 hours sitting at a stretch, no full‑day field work. Life waiver app submitted Jan 2025; Sun Life acknowledged Jan 17, wants more from Grand River specialist; we’re still paying life premiums.

• My own file: stress‑related burnout in Oct 2023, week off, James scrambling for emergency leave from Grand River Collegiate to watch the kids. Dr. Nguyen at the Kitchener Downtown Community Health Centre says prognosis is good if I keep to 45–50 hr weeks and monthly check‑ins through Dec 2025. No life waiver, just me trying not to repeat history.

Remember: these are people and families, not just “experience and rates provided.”

metadata.title: Things I don’t say in broker meetings
